1:
"Masters, give unto your servants that which is just and equal; knowing that ye also have a Master in heaven." The witnesses note the charge to masters to deal justly, knowing their own Master in heaven. On "masters, give unto your servants that which is just and equal," the grammatical voices note the duty. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's charge to masters (completing the household duties begun in ch. 3): "give unto your servants (slaves) that which is just (righteous, fair) and equal (equitable, fair-dealing)" — masters owe their servants justice and fairness, not arbitrary or harsh treatment.

On "just and equal," the witnesses note the breadth. Barnes and Calvin note that "just and equal" requires masters to render what is right — fair provision, fair treatment, fair reward; the master is bound by justice no less than the servant by obedience.

On "ye also have a Master in heaven," the puritan Henry notes the motive: masters are to remember that "ye also have a Master (Lord) in heaven" — they too are servants, accountable to God, who will judge their treatment of those under them; this checks all tyranny. The verse displays the charge to masters to deal justly.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's charge "give unto your servants that which is just (fair) and equal (equitable)," masters owing justice not harshness (Barnes, Robertson); "just and equal" requiring fair provision, treatment, and reward, the master bound by justice (Barnes, Calvin); "ye also have a Master in heaven," masters accountable to God, who judges their treatment, checking tyranny (Henry). The verse displays just masters. The verse marks the charge: just and equal. The verse displays the duty: fairness to servants. The verse displays the motive: a Master in heaven. The verse displays the check: accountability. The charge displays masters dealing justly under God. The verse displays the masters: charged. The verse displays the servants: dealt with justly. The verse displays the Master: in heaven. The charge to masters to deal justly, knowing their own Master in heaven, displays the mutual obligation of the Christian household: masters are to render their servants what is just and equitable — fair in provision, treatment, and reward — remembering that they too have a Master in heaven to whom they must answer. The verse displays the duty of masters, closing the household code and opening the call to prayer (v.2).
2:
"Continue in prayer, and watch in the same with thanksgiving;" The brief verse notes the call to persevere in watchful, thankful prayer. The grammatical voices note the perseverance. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's charge: "continue (proskartereite — persevere, be steadfast, devote yourselves constantly) in prayer" — believers are to be constant and persistent in prayer, never giving it up.

On "watch in the same," the witnesses note the alertness. Barnes and Calvin note the added duty: "watch (be watchful, alert, vigilant) in the same" — prayer is to be accompanied by watchfulness, an attentive, wakeful spirit, not drowsy or careless; watching against distraction and for answers.

On "with thanksgiving," the puritan Henry notes the spirit: prayer is to be joined "with thanksgiving" — gratitude for mercies received is to mingle with every petition; thankfulness is the constant companion of prayer. The verse displays the call to persevere in watchful, thankful prayer.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's charge "continue (persevere, be steadfast) in prayer," constant and persistent, never giving up (Barnes, Robertson); "watch (be vigilant, alert) in the same," prayer joined with a wakeful, attentive spirit against distraction (Barnes, Calvin); "with thanksgiving," gratitude mingling with every petition (Henry). The verse displays watchful prayer. The verse marks the perseverance: continue in prayer. The verse displays the watchfulness: vigilant. The verse displays the gratitude: with thanksgiving. The verse displays the spirit: alert and thankful. The call displays believers persevering in watchful, thankful prayer. The verse displays the praying: constant. The verse displays the watching: alert. The verse displays the thanks: joined. The call to persevere in watchful, thankful prayer displays the heart of the believer's devotion: prayer is to be constant and persistent, accompanied by a vigilant, wakeful spirit, and mingled always with thanksgiving for mercies received. The verse displays the discipline of prayer, with a request for intercession (vv.3-4). The three things here joined together belong inseparably to the life of devotion, for steadfast perseverance keeps the soul at its post when feeling fails, watchfulness keeps it awake and attentive against the drowsiness and distraction that so easily creep in, and thanksgiving keeps it from the querulous, demanding spirit that turns prayer into mere complaint; and the apostle, who himself prayed without ceasing, would have the whole church marked by this same constant, alert, and grateful approach to God.
3:
"Withal praying also for us, that God would open unto us a door of utterance, to speak the mystery of Christ, for which I am also in bonds:" The witnesses note Paul's request for prayer that a door of utterance be opened. On "praying also for us, that God would open unto us a door of utterance," the grammatical voices note the request. Barnes and Robertson note Paul asks their intercession for himself and his fellow-workers: "praying also for us, that God would open unto us a door of utterance (a door for the word, an opportunity to preach)" — that God would give opportunity and freedom to proclaim the gospel.

On "to speak the mystery of Christ," the witnesses note the theme. Barnes and Calvin note the content: "to speak the mystery of Christ" — the once-hidden, now-revealed truth of Christ and the gospel, especially the inclusion of the Gentiles; this is what Paul desires opportunity to preach.

On "for which I am also in bonds," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the cost: it is "for which (the mystery, the gospel) I am also in bonds" — Paul's very imprisonment is for preaching this mystery; yet even in bonds he seeks opportunity to proclaim it. The verse displays Paul's request for prayer that a door be opened.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul asks their intercession — "praying for us, that God would open unto us a door of utterance (an opportunity to preach)," freedom to proclaim the gospel (Barnes, Robertson); the content "the mystery of Christ," the once-hidden now-revealed truth of the gospel, especially the Gentiles (Barnes, Calvin); "for which I am also in bonds," his imprisonment being for this very gospel (Wesley). The verse displays the door of utterance. The verse marks the request: pray for us. The verse displays the petition: a door of utterance. The verse displays the theme: the mystery of Christ. The verse displays the cost: in bonds. The request displays Paul seeking opportunity to preach. The verse displays the prayer: for him. The verse displays the door: opened. The verse displays the bonds: for the gospel. Paul's request for prayer that a door of utterance be opened displays his single passion even in prison: he asks the Colossians to pray that God would give him opportunity and freedom to proclaim the mystery of Christ — the very gospel for which he lies in bonds. The verse displays the apostle's longing to preach, that he may make it manifest (v.4).
4:
"That I may make it manifest, as I ought to speak." The brief verse notes Paul's desire to make the gospel plain as he ought. The grammatical voices note the aim. Barnes and Robertson note the purpose of the open door (v.3): "that I may make it (the mystery of Christ) manifest (clear, plain, openly known)" — Paul's desire is not merely to speak but to make the gospel clearly understood.

On "as I ought to speak," the witnesses note the obligation. Barnes and Calvin note the standard: "as I ought (as it is my duty, as is fitting) to speak" — Paul feels bound to declare the gospel plainly and faithfully; he asks prayer that he may discharge this duty as he should.

The Reformed Calvin notes the dependence: even the great apostle asks prayer that he may speak rightly — for the ability to make the gospel manifest is God's gift, not his own attainment; he leans on the prayers of the church. The verse displays Paul's desire to make the gospel plain as he ought.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the purpose of the open door — "that I may make it (the mystery) manifest (clear, plain)," desiring the gospel clearly understood (Barnes, Robertson); "as I ought (as is my duty) to speak," bound to declare it plainly and faithfully (Barnes, Calvin); even the apostle asking prayer to speak rightly, leaning on the church (Calvin). The verse displays making it manifest. The verse marks the aim: make it manifest. The verse displays the clarity: plain and clear. The verse displays the obligation: as I ought. The verse displays the dependence: on prayer. The desire displays Paul seeking to preach the gospel plainly. The verse displays the manifesting: clear. The verse displays the duty: to speak. The verse displays the dependence: on God. Paul's desire to make the gospel plain as he ought displays the aim of his requested prayer: that, given the open door, he may make the mystery of Christ clearly known — declaring it plainly and faithfully as his duty requires, leaning even so on the prayers of the church. The verse displays the apostle's care to preach clearly, turning to the believers' walk (vv.5-6).
5:
"Walk in wisdom toward them that are without, redeeming the time." The witnesses note the call to walk wisely toward outsiders, redeeming the time. On "walk in wisdom toward them that are without," the grammatical voices note the conduct. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's charge concerning conduct before the unconverted: "walk (live, conduct yourselves) in wisdom toward them that are without (those outside the church, the unbelievers)" — believers are to behave wisely and prudently in the sight of those not yet in the faith.

On "them that are without," the eastern Chrysostom notes the witness. Chrysostom and Calvin note that the conduct of believers toward outsiders is a witness — wise, blameless behaviour adorns the gospel and may win them, while folly or scandal repels; much depends on how the church appears to the world.

On "redeeming the time," the grammatical-historical note the diligence: "redeeming the time (buying up the opportunity, making the most of every occasion)" — believers are to seize every opportunity, especially to do good and commend the gospel to outsiders. The verse displays the call to walk wisely toward outsiders.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's charge "walk (conduct yourselves) in wisdom toward them that are without (the unbelievers)," behaving wisely before those outside the faith (Barnes, Robertson); the conduct a witness, wise behaviour adorning the gospel and winning them, folly repelling (Chrysostom, Calvin); "redeeming the time (buying up the opportunity)," seizing every occasion to commend the gospel (grammatical-historical). The verse displays wise conduct. The verse marks the walk: in wisdom. The verse displays the audience: them without. The verse displays the witness: adorning the gospel. The verse displays the diligence: redeeming the time. The call displays believers walking wisely before the world. The verse displays the wisdom: in conduct. The verse displays the outsiders: watching. The verse displays the time: redeemed. The call to walk wisely toward outsiders, redeeming the time, displays the believer's witness before the world: he is to conduct himself with wisdom and prudence in the sight of the unconverted — that his behaviour may adorn the gospel — seizing every opportunity to commend it. The verse displays the wise walk before the world, completed by gracious speech (v.6).
6:
"Let your speech be alway with grace, seasoned with salt, that ye may know how ye ought to answer every man." The witnesses note the call to gracious, well-seasoned speech. On "let your speech be alway with grace," the grammatical voices note the charge. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's rule for the tongue: "let your speech (conversation) be alway (at all times) with grace (gracious, pleasant, winning, full of spiritual benefit)" — the believer's speech is to be always kind, edifying, and gracious.

On "seasoned with salt," the witnesses note the figure. Barnes and Calvin note the image: "seasoned with salt" — as salt gives savour and preserves from corruption, so speech is to be wholesome, savoury, and preserving; not insipid, not corrupt, but with point and wholesomeness; some add the sense of wit and good sense, free from all that is flat or rotten.

On "that ye may know how ye ought to answer every man," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the aim: gracious, savoury speech equips the believer to "know how ye ought to answer every man" — to give a fitting, wise reply to each person, whatever his question or need. The verse displays the call to gracious, well-seasoned speech.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's rule "let your speech be alway (at all times) with grace (gracious, winning, edifying)," always kind and gracious (Barnes, Robertson); "seasoned with salt," wholesome and savoury, preserving from corruption, with point not insipid or rotten (Barnes, Calvin); "that ye may know how ye ought to answer every man," equipped to give a fitting, wise reply to each (Wesley). The verse displays gracious speech. The verse marks the grace: always gracious. The verse displays the savour: seasoned with salt. The verse displays the wholesomeness: preserving. The verse displays the aim: answer every man. The call displays believers speaking with grace and savour. The verse displays the speech: gracious. The verse displays the salt: seasoning. The verse displays the answer: fitting. The call to gracious, well-seasoned speech displays the discipline of the Christian tongue: the believer's words are to be always gracious and edifying, savoury and wholesome like salt — that he may be ready to give a fitting and wise answer to every person. The verse displays the grace of speech, closing the exhortation and opening the greetings (vv.7-18).
7:
"All my state shall Tychicus declare unto you, who is a beloved brother, and a faithful minister and fellowservant in the Lord:" The witnesses note Paul commending Tychicus, the beloved brother and faithful minister. On "all my state shall Tychicus declare unto you," the grammatical voices note the messenger. Barnes and Robertson note Paul introduces the bearer of the letter: "all my state (my affairs, how I fare) shall Tychicus declare (make known) unto you" — Tychicus would tell them in person all about Paul's circumstances.

On "a beloved brother, and a faithful minister and fellowservant," the witnesses note the threefold commendation. Barnes and JFB note the warm titles: Tychicus is "a beloved brother (dear in the faith), a faithful minister (diakonos — a trusty servant of the gospel), and fellowservant (syndoulos — a fellow-slave of Christ) in the Lord" — high praise of a trusted companion.

The latin-fathers Tertullian notes the office: such faithful ministers and fellow-servants were the channels through whom the apostle's word and care reached the churches; Tychicus bears both the letter and Paul's heart. The verse displays Paul commending Tychicus.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul introduces the bearer — "all my state (my affairs) shall Tychicus declare unto you," telling them in person of Paul's circumstances (Barnes, Robertson); the threefold commendation "a beloved brother, a faithful minister (trusty servant of the gospel), and fellowservant (fellow-slave of Christ) in the Lord," high praise of a trusted companion (Barnes, JFB); such ministers the channels of the apostle's word and care (Tertullian). The verse displays the commendation. The verse marks the messenger: Tychicus. The verse displays the errand: declare my state. The verse displays the titles: beloved, faithful, fellowservant. The verse displays the trust: a trusted companion. The commendation displays Paul honouring his faithful messenger. The verse displays the brother: beloved. The verse displays the minister: faithful. The verse displays the fellowservant: in the Lord. Paul commending Tychicus, the beloved brother and faithful minister, displays the honour he gives his trusted companion: Tychicus, the bearer of the letter, would declare to them all of Paul's affairs — a beloved brother, a faithful minister, and a fellow-servant in the Lord. The verse displays the trusted messenger, sent with Onesimus to comfort them (vv.8-9).
8:
"Whom I have sent unto you for the same purpose, that he might know your estate, and comfort your hearts;" The brief verse notes Tychicus sent to learn their estate and comfort their hearts. The grammatical voices note the errand. Barnes and Robertson note the double purpose of Tychicus's mission: "whom I have sent unto you for the same purpose, that he might know your estate (learn your circumstances, how you fare)" — to bring back word of the Colossians' condition to Paul.

On "comfort your hearts," the witnesses note the other aim. Barnes and Calvin note the second purpose: "and comfort (encourage, console, strengthen) your hearts" — Tychicus was to bring them encouragement, cheering them with news of Paul and with his own ministry; his coming was for their consolation.

The grammatical-historical note the mutual care: the mission served both ends — Paul would learn of them, and they would be comforted; the messenger bound apostle and church together in mutual concern. The verse displays Tychicus sent to learn their estate and comfort their hearts.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the double purpose — "whom I have sent... that he might know your estate (learn how you fare)," bringing back word of their condition (Barnes, Robertson); "and comfort (encourage, console) your hearts," bringing them encouragement with news of Paul (Barnes, Calvin); the mission serving both ends, binding apostle and church (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the errand. The verse marks the sending: Tychicus sent. The verse displays the first aim: know your estate. The verse displays the second: comfort your hearts. The verse displays the mutuality: binding them. The errand displays Tychicus serving Paul and the church. The verse displays the knowing: their estate. The verse displays the comfort: their hearts. The verse displays the care: mutual. Tychicus sent to learn their estate and comfort their hearts displays the mutual care binding Paul and the Colossians: the messenger was sent both to bring back word of their circumstances and to encourage and console them — serving the love that flowed between the imprisoned apostle and his distant church. The verse displays the comforting errand, shared with Onesimus (v.9).
9:
"With Onesimus, a faithful and beloved brother, who is one of you. They shall make known unto you all things which are done here." The brief verse notes Onesimus, now a faithful brother, sent with Tychicus. The grammatical voices note the companion. Barnes and Robertson note that Tychicus is accompanied by "Onesimus, a faithful and beloved brother" — the same Onesimus who was the runaway slave of Philemon, now converted and sent back, commended by Paul as a true brother (cf. the Epistle to Philemon).

On "who is one of you," the witnesses note his origin. Barnes and Ellicott note that Onesimus "is one of you" — a Colossian, belonging to their own community; though once a fugitive slave, he is now received as one of their own, a beloved brother in Christ.

On "they shall make known unto you all things which are done here," the grammatical-historical note the errand: the two together "shall make known unto you all things which are done here" — Tychicus and Onesimus would report all of Paul's situation at Rome. The verse displays Onesimus, now a faithful brother, sent with Tychicus.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Tychicus is accompanied by "Onesimus, a faithful and beloved brother," the once-runaway slave of Philemon, now converted and commended as a true brother (Barnes, Robertson); "who is one of you," a Colossian, received as their own though once a fugitive (Barnes, Ellicott); the two "shall make known... all things which are done here," reporting Paul's situation (grammatical-historical). The verse displays Onesimus the brother. The verse marks the companion: Onesimus. The verse displays the change: now a faithful brother. The verse displays the origin: one of you. The verse displays the errand: make known all things. The verse displays the grace transforming the runaway slave. The verse displays the Onesimus: beloved. The verse displays the brother: faithful. The verse displays the report: all things. Onesimus, now a faithful brother, sent with Tychicus, displays the transforming grace of the gospel: the once-runaway slave is now a faithful and beloved brother, one of the Colossians' own, sent back with Tychicus to make known all of Paul's circumstances. The verse displays the converted slave honoured, before the salutations of Paul's companions (vv.10-14).
10:
"Aristarchus my fellowprisoner saluteth you, and Marcus, sister's son to Barnabas, (touching whom ye received commandments: if he come unto you, receive him;)" The witnesses note the greetings of Aristarchus and Marcus. On "Aristarchus my fellowprisoner," the grammatical voices note the first. Barnes and Robertson note the first to send greeting: "Aristarchus my fellowprisoner (synaichmalotos — fellow-captive)" — a companion of Paul (Acts 19:29; 27:2) who shared his imprisonment, sending his salutation to the Colossians.

On "Marcus, sister's son to Barnabas," the witnesses note the second. Barnes and Henry note "Marcus (John Mark), sister's son (cousin) to Barnabas" — the same Mark who had once departed from Paul (Acts 15:37-39), now reconciled and again a fellow-worker; the relationship to Barnabas is noted.

On "if he come unto you, receive him," the grammatical-historical note the commendation: "touching whom ye received commandments: if he come unto you, receive him" — Paul instructs them to welcome Mark, a full restoration of one who had once been the occasion of a sharp parting. The verse displays the greetings of Aristarchus and Marcus.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "Aristarchus my fellowprisoner (fellow-captive)," a companion sharing Paul's imprisonment, sending greeting (Barnes, Robertson); "Marcus (John Mark), sister's son (cousin) to Barnabas," once departed from Paul, now reconciled and a fellow-worker (Barnes, Henry); "if he come unto you, receive him," Paul commending Mark's welcome, a full restoration (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the greetings. The verse marks the first: Aristarchus. The verse displays the captivity: fellowprisoner. The verse displays the second: Marcus. The verse displays the commendation: receive him. The greetings display Paul's reconciled companions. The verse displays the Aristarchus: fellow-captive. The verse displays the Marcus: restored. The verse displays the welcome: commended. The greetings of Aristarchus and Marcus display the fellowship around the imprisoned apostle: Aristarchus, his fellow-prisoner, sends greeting, as does Mark, the cousin of Barnabas — once the occasion of a sharp parting, now reconciled, whom Paul commends to their welcome. The verse displays the greetings of restored companions, with Justus (v.11). What is most touching in this little cluster of greetings is the restoration it quietly records, for the Mark here so warmly commended is the very man whose earlier desertion had once provoked so sharp a contention that Paul and Barnabas parted company over him; yet now, years later, the breach is healed, the deserter is a trusted fellow-worker again, and the apostle is at pains to secure him a welcome, a standing witness that failure need not be final in the service of Christ.
11:
"And Jesus, which is called Justus, who are of the circumcision. These only are my fellowworkers unto the kingdom of God, which have been a comfort unto me." The brief verse notes Justus and the Jewish fellow-workers who comforted Paul. The grammatical voices note the third name. Barnes and Robertson note the third of this group: "Jesus, which is called Justus" — otherwise unknown, bearing the common Jewish name Jesus (Joshua) with the Latin surname Justus.

On "who are of the circumcision," the witnesses note their origin. Barnes and Calvin note that these three (Aristarchus, Mark, Justus) "are of the circumcision" — Jewish believers; of all the Jewish Christians, only these few stood with Paul as fellow-workers, the rest largely opposing or holding aloof.

On "these only... have been a comfort unto me," the eastern Chrysostom notes the consolation: "these only are my fellowworkers unto the kingdom of God, which have been a comfort (consolation, encouragement) unto me" — amid much opposition, these few faithful Jewish workers were a real comfort to the imprisoned apostle. The verse displays Justus and the Jewish fellow-workers who comforted Paul.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the third name "Jesus, which is called Justus," otherwise unknown, with a Jewish name and Latin surname (Barnes, Robertson); these three "of the circumcision," Jewish believers, of whom only these few stood with Paul (Barnes, Calvin); "these only... have been a comfort unto me," a real consolation amid opposition (Chrysostom). The verse displays the comforting workers. The verse marks the name: Justus. The verse displays the origin: of the circumcision. The verse displays the fewness: these only. The verse displays the comfort: to Paul. The greeting displays the few faithful Jewish workers comforting Paul. The verse displays the Justus: greeting. The verse displays the circumcision: their stock. The verse displays the comfort: given. Justus and the Jewish fellow-workers who comforted Paul display the consolation he found amid opposition: of the believers of the circumcision, only these few — Aristarchus, Mark, and Justus — stood with him as fellow-workers for the kingdom, and they had been a real comfort to the imprisoned apostle. The verse displays the faithful few, joined by Epaphras (vv.12-13).
12:
"Epaphras, who is one of you, a servant of Christ, saluteth you, always labouring fervently for you in prayers, that ye may stand perfect and complete in all the will of God." The witnesses note Epaphras wrestling in prayer that they stand perfect in God's will. On "Epaphras, who is one of you, a servant of Christ," the grammatical voices note the man. Barnes and Robertson note Epaphras, the founder or evangelist of the Colossian church (1:7): "who is one of you (a Colossian), a servant (bondservant) of Christ" — their own beloved minister, now with Paul, sending greeting.

On "always labouring fervently for you in prayers," the witnesses note his intercession. Barnes and Chrysostom note the striking phrase: "always labouring fervently (agonizomenos — striving, wrestling, agonizing, as in a contest) for you in prayers" — Epaphras prayed for the Colossians with intense, wrestling earnestness, an athlete's struggle in intercession.

On "that ye may stand perfect and complete in all the will of God," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the aim: he wrestled "that ye may stand perfect (mature, complete) and complete (fully assured, filled) in all the will of God" — that they might be established, mature, and fully assured in all God wills. The verse displays Epaphras wrestling in prayer for them.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Epaphras, the Colossians' own minister (1:7), "one of you, a servant of Christ," now with Paul (Barnes, Robertson); "always labouring fervently (wrestling, agonizing, as in a contest) for you in prayers," interceding with intense earnestness (Barnes, Chrysostom); "that ye may stand perfect (mature) and complete (fully assured) in all the will of God," established and mature (Wesley). The verse displays wrestling prayer. The verse marks the man: Epaphras. The verse displays the labour: wrestling in prayer. The verse displays the intensity: fervently. The verse displays the aim: perfect and complete. The intercession displays Epaphras agonizing for their maturity. The verse displays the Epaphras: praying. The verse displays the wrestling: fervent. The verse displays the standing: perfect. Epaphras wrestling in prayer that they stand perfect in God's will displays the labour of true intercession: the Colossians' own minister strove and agonized in prayer for them, as an athlete in a contest, that they might stand mature, complete, and fully assured in all the will of God. The verse displays the wrestling prayer of Epaphras, witnessed by Paul (v.13).
13:
"For I bear him record, that he hath a great zeal for you, and them that are in Laodicea, and them in Hierapolis." The brief verse notes Paul's witness to the zeal of Epaphras for three churches. The grammatical voices note the testimony. Barnes and Robertson note Paul adds his own witness to Epaphras's labour: "I bear him record (I testify on his behalf), that he hath a great zeal (much labour, deep concern, earnest toil) for you" — Paul vouches for the intensity of Epaphras's care.

On "and them that are in Laodicea, and them in Hierapolis," the witnesses note the wider care. Barnes and JFB note that Epaphras's zeal extended beyond Colosse to the neighbouring churches: "them that are in Laodicea, and them in Hierapolis" — the three cities of the Lycus valley, all objects of his prayerful concern.

The grammatical-historical note the breadth: Epaphras's intercession was not narrow but embraced the whole region; his pastoral heart took in all three churches. The verse displays Paul's witness to the zeal of Epaphras.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul adds his witness — "I bear him record (testify), that he hath a great zeal (deep concern, earnest toil) for you," vouching for Epaphras's care (Barnes, Robertson); his zeal extending to "them in Laodicea and Hierapolis," the three cities of the Lycus valley (Barnes, JFB); his intercession embracing the whole region (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the witness to his zeal. The verse marks the testimony: I bear record. The verse displays the zeal: great. The verse displays the breadth: three churches. The verse displays the region: the Lycus valley. The record displays Epaphras's wide pastoral zeal. The verse displays the zeal: attested. The verse displays the churches: three. The verse displays the care: wide. Paul's witness to the zeal of Epaphras for three churches displays the breadth of his pastoral heart: Paul testifies that Epaphras labours with deep concern not only for Colosse but for Laodicea and Hierapolis — the whole Lycus valley embraced in his prayerful zeal. The verse displays the wide care of Epaphras, before the greetings of Luke and Demas (v.14).
14:
"Luke, the beloved physician, and Demas, greet you." The brief verse notes the greetings of Luke and Demas. The grammatical voices note Luke. Barnes and Robertson note the greeting of "Luke, the beloved physician" — the writer of the Gospel and Acts, Paul's faithful companion and friend, here described by his profession; a beloved fellow-worker who stayed with Paul to the end (2 Timothy 4:11).

On "the beloved physician," the witnesses note the affection. Barnes and Calvin note the warm title "beloved" — Luke was dear to Paul, his loyal companion in travels and imprisonment; his medical skill perhaps a comfort to the often-afflicted apostle.

On "and Demas," the grammatical-historical note the contrast: "Demas" sends greeting too — but his name is given without any commendation, and he would later forsake Paul, "having loved this present world" (2 Timothy 4:10); the bare mention here stands in poignant contrast to his later defection. The verse displays the greetings of Luke and Demas.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "Luke, the beloved physician," the writer of the Gospel and Acts, Paul's faithful companion to the end, described by his profession (Barnes, Robertson); the warm "beloved," Luke dear to Paul, his loyal companion (Barnes, Calvin); "and Demas," greeting without commendation, who would later forsake Paul for the world (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the two greetings. The verse marks the first: Luke. The verse displays the title: beloved physician. The verse displays the affection: dear to Paul. The verse displays the second: Demas. The greetings display the faithful Luke and the later-faithless Demas. The verse displays the Luke: beloved. The verse displays the physician: faithful. The verse displays the Demas: bare. The greetings of Luke and Demas display the company around Paul and a poignant contrast: Luke, the beloved physician, his loyal companion to the end, and Demas — here greeting without praise, who would afterward forsake the apostle, having loved this present world. The verse displays the two companions, before the greeting to Laodicea (v.15). The bare naming of Demas, set without a word of praise beside the warm commendation of Luke, gains a sombre weight when read in the light of what came afterwards, for this is the same Demas of whom the apostle would one day write, with evident grief, that he had forsaken him, having loved this present world; and so the two names standing here together, the beloved physician who stayed to the end and the companion who would not, become a quiet parable of the perseverance and the apostasy that may begin from the same outward fellowship.
15:
"Salute the brethren which are in Laodicea, and Nymphas, and the church which is in his house." The witnesses note the greeting to Laodicea and the church in the house — with the Nymphas textual question. On "salute the brethren which are in Laodicea," the grammatical voices note the neighbouring church. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's greeting to the nearby church: "the brethren which are in Laodicea" — the Christians of the neighbouring city, closely bound to Colosse, to whom Paul sends his salutation.

On "Nymphas, and the church which is in his house," interpreters note a textual variant. The grammatical-historical Ellicott and Robertson note the variety of reading: the name may be masculine "Nymphas" (with "his house," D E K L) or feminine "Nympha" (with "her house," B 67), while the best-attested reading is "their house" (Aleph A C P) — Robertson concluding it is not possible to tell whether "her" or "his." Of this person, host of a house-church, nothing more is known.

On "the church which is in his house," the Reformed Calvin notes the household-church: from this one household a rule is laid down for all Christian homes — that they be so many "little churches," each trained in the fear of the Lord under holy discipline. The verse displays the greeting to Laodicea and the house-church.

The witnesses agree the substance: Paul greets "the brethren which are in Laodicea," the neighbouring church bound to Colosse, and the house-church gathered around Nymphas; such house-churches, before separate buildings existed, met in the homes of leading believers, and Calvin draws from it that every Christian household should be a little church under holy discipline (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin). And they note the textual question: the name and pronoun vary — masculine "Nymphas"/"his house" (D E K L), feminine "Nympha"/"her house" (B 67), or "their house" (the best reading, Aleph A C P) — Robertson holding it cannot be determined. The verse displays the greeting and the house-church. The verse marks the church: in Laodicea. The verse displays the host: Nymphas. The verse displays the variant: his, her, or their house. The verse displays the household-church: a little church. The greeting displays the neighbouring church and the church in the house. The verse displays the Laodicea: greeted. The verse displays the Nymphas: host. The verse displays the house: a church. The greeting to Laodicea and the church in the house displays the fellowship of neighbouring churches and a textual crux: Paul salutes the brethren at Laodicea and the house-church of Nymphas — whose name and household the manuscripts variously give as his, her, or their — a household that, Calvin urges, every Christian home should imitate as a little church. The verse displays the household-church, with a charge to exchange epistles (v.16).
16:
"And when this epistle is read among you, cause that it be read also in the church of the Laodiceans; and that ye likewise read the epistle from Laodicea." The witnesses note the charge to exchange epistles — and the crux of the epistle from Laodicea. On "when this epistle is read among you, cause that it be read also in the church of the Laodiceans," the grammatical voices note the public reading. Barnes and Robertson note Paul directs the public reading and interchange of his letters: "when this epistle is read among you (publicly, to the assembled church), cause that it be read also in the church of the Laodiceans" — the counsel being equally needful for both neighbouring churches; this shows how the apostolic letters were first published and came to be widely received.

On "the epistle from Laodicea," interpreters divide sharply. The grammatical-historical Ellicott and Robertson hold it is most likely our Epistle to the Ephesians — an encyclical (circular) letter to the churches of Asia, one copy reaching Laodicea and to be passed on; Marcion expressly called Ephesians the letter "to the Laodiceans." Others (allowed by Clarke) hold it a genuine Pauline letter to Laodicea now lost. The Reformed Calvin, with the wesleyan-arminian Wesley, reads "the epistle from Laodicea" as a letter sent from Laodicea to Paul, whose perusal would profit the Colossians — "not to Laodicea" (Wesley). All alike reject the extant Latin "Epistle to the Laodiceans" as a forgery — Calvin a "gross imposture" by "some worthless person," Ellicott "obviously a forgery," Jerome (in Clarke) "exploded by all."

The witnesses note the purpose: the letters were to circulate for the common profit of the churches. The verse displays the charge to exchange epistles.

The witnesses agree the charge: Paul directs the public reading of his letter and its interchange with Laodicea, the counsel needful for both — a window into how the apostolic letters were published and canonized (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott). But "the epistle from Laodicea" divides them: Ellicott and Robertson identify it with Ephesians the encyclical (Marcion calling it "to the Laodiceans"); some (Clarke allows) suppose a lost Pauline letter to Laodicea; Calvin and Wesley read it as a letter sent from Laodicea to Paul. All reject the extant Latin forgery. The verse displays the crux. The verse marks the reading: public, in both churches. The verse displays the interchange: this and the Laodicean letter. The verse displays the debate: Ephesians, a lost letter, or a letter to Paul. The verse displays the rejected: the Latin forgery. The charge displays the letters circulating for common profit. The verse displays the epistle: exchanged. The verse displays the Laodicea: its letter. The verse displays the forgery: rejected. The charge to exchange epistles, and the crux of the epistle from Laodicea, display the early circulation of Paul's letters and a famous puzzle: his letter was to be read at Laodicea and theirs at Colosse — "the epistle from Laodicea" being identified as Ephesians the encyclical (Ellicott, Robertson), a lost Pauline letter (some), or a letter sent to Paul (Calvin, Wesley), while the extant Latin forgery is rejected by all. The verse displays the interchange of the epistles, before the charge to Archippus (v.17).
17:
"And say to Archippus, Take heed to the ministry which thou hast received in the Lord, that thou fulfil it." The witnesses note the charge to Archippus to fulfil his ministry. On "say to Archippus, Take heed to the ministry," the grammatical voices note the exhortation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's message to a minister of the church: "say to Archippus (named also in Philemon 2, perhaps Philemon's son), Take heed (blepe — keep an eye on, give attention to) the ministry (diakonia — the office of ministering in the gospel) which thou hast received in the Lord" — an exhortation to faithful diligence in his office.

On whether it implies rebuke, the witnesses differ mildly. Barnes and Clarke note that the charge need not imply that Archippus had been negligent; rather it is an encouragement to diligence and zeal, fitting for any minister amid false teachers — though Calvin and Gill suppose it does gently stir up one who may have grown cold.

On "that thou fulfil it," the Reformed Calvin notes the manner of the charge: that it is given through the whole church — Paul would have the Colossians themselves urge their pastor on, for ministers, though endowed with authority, are not exempt from being admonished by the flock; he is to "fulfil (fully discharge, make full proof of)" his ministry. The verse displays the charge to Archippus to fulfil his ministry.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's message — "say to Archippus, Take heed (give attention to) the ministry (office) which thou hast received in the Lord," an exhortation to faithful diligence (Barnes, Robertson); the charge perhaps not a rebuke but an encouragement to zeal amid false teachers, though some take it as a gentle stirring (Barnes, Clarke, Calvin); given through the whole church, that they urge their pastor on, that he "fulfil (make full proof of)" it (Calvin). The verse displays the charge to Archippus. The verse marks the man: Archippus. The verse displays the exhortation: take heed. The verse displays the office: the ministry. The verse displays the manner: through the church. The charge displays Archippus urged to faithfulness. The verse displays the ministry: received. The verse displays the heed: charged. The verse displays the fulfilment: required. The charge to Archippus to fulfil his ministry displays the diligence required of God's servants: Paul bids him take heed to the ministry he received in the Lord and fully discharge it — a charge sent through the whole church, that the flock too might stir up their pastor to faithfulness. The verse displays the call to fulfil the ministry, before Paul's closing salutation (v.18).
18:
"The salutation by the hand of me Paul. Remember my bonds. Grace be with you. Amen." The witnesses note Paul's closing salutation in his own hand, his bonds, and the benediction. On "the salutation by the hand of me Paul," the grammatical voices note the autograph. Barnes and Robertson note that Paul, who dictated the letter, now adds the closing in his own hand: "the salutation by the hand of me Paul" — his personal signature, the mark of authenticity he gave his letters (2 Thessalonians 3:17), guarding against forgery.

On "remember my bonds," the witnesses note the appeal. Barnes and Ellicott note the touching request: "remember my bonds (my chains, my imprisonment)" — let them not forget that he writes as a prisoner for the gospel; the appeal both for their prayers and sympathy and as a seal of his testimony, the hand that wrote being chained.

On "grace be with you. Amen," the grammatical-historical note the benediction: Paul closes, as ever, with "grace be with you" — invoking the free favour of God on the whole church, sealing the letter with "Amen." The verse displays Paul's closing salutation, his bonds, and the benediction.

The witnesses agree: Paul adds the closing in his own hand — "the salutation by the hand of me Paul," his personal signature and mark of authenticity (2 Thessalonians 3:17), guarding against forgery (Barnes, Robertson); the touching "remember my bonds," that they not forget he writes as a prisoner for the gospel, an appeal for prayer and a seal of his testimony (Barnes, Ellicott); "grace be with you. Amen," the closing benediction invoking God's free favour on all (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the closing salutation. The verse marks the hand: of Paul. The verse displays the autograph: his signature. The verse displays the appeal: remember my bonds. The verse displays the benediction: grace be with you. The close displays Paul signing, appealing, and blessing. The verse displays the salutation: his own. The verse displays the bonds: remembered. The verse displays the grace: invoked. Paul's closing salutation in his own hand, his bonds, and the benediction display the seal of the epistle: the apostle signs the letter himself, asks them to remember his chains, and invokes the grace of God upon them all — sealing with Amen the epistle to the Colossians, with its exaltation of the supremacy and all-sufficiency of Christ.
